name: Poems for the Becoming-Beggar
description: A small, growing collection of poems for contemplative practice — drawn from retreat, from daughterhood, from the cracks where ordinary certainty has given way. Each card holds one poem. Draw a card and let the poem say what it says. Intended as a shared grammar: contributions welcome from practitioners whose poems belong here.

attribution.source_author: Multiple contributors — each poem attributed per item
attribution.note: Each poem is contributed by its author under CC BY-SA 4.0. Attribution preserved per item. Working title after [L]'s poem 'Let yourself become a beggar' — the author may rename once the collection takes its final shape.

sections.Poem:
Let yourself become a beggar
A mendicant
An ignorant and wandering fool
Who knows nothing

And needs everything

Let your defenses be shattered,
Let your walls come crumbling down.

Your competency is what stands between you

And all you long for.
sections.The poet's note: I shared this poem on retreat and share it again with you now. I keep reminding myself that not knowing and not being capable leaves room for grace.
sections.What this poem teaches:
Competency is not a neutral quality. Competency is a wall. The things you most long for — grace, presence, being met, being held by what is larger than you — arrive in the gap competency closes. The practice is not to become incompetent; the practice is to let the competence you have stop functioning as a defense against what wants to reach you.

Beggardom is not a state of lack. Beggardom is a posture: I know nothing, I need everything, and I am available to be given what I cannot produce myself. This is the posture the contemplative traditions have been asking of practitioners for millennia, and it is the posture modern life trains out of us most thoroughly.
sections.A prompt for drawing this card: Where in your life is your competence standing between you and what you long for? Not: where are you incompetent? But: where has your capacity to handle, to manage, to solve, to perform become the exact thing that keeps you from being reached?

attribution.author: [L]
attribution.context: Shared at a Śaiva Tantra retreat, California 2026; shared again in the post-retreat integration group on April 24, 2026
attribution.license: CC BY-SA 4.0, with the poet's permission to include

items.name: A Song That Loves You Back

sections.Poem:
The crash, the crush, that awful sound —
it planted something in the ground.
And what grew up between the cracks
is this: a song that loves you back.
sections.The poet's note: I wrote it a year or two ago, after a day that went badly in a small street-level way — a truck came too soon, a pile of things we had meant to give away went instead to a crushing place, and my daughter screamed down the block after it until the driver stopped, though by then there was nothing he could do. We sat on the curb together. I could not fix what had happened. I could only hold her. I wrote the song to try to find a shape for what had happened. The shape is not an argument. The shape is a refrain. I have been singing it to her for long enough that it has become, in its small way, what I mean when I mean practice.
sections.What this poem teaches:
The composting chapter. The crash is real. The crush is real. The ground receives what lands in it. The cracks are not where the story ends; the cracks are where the different growth becomes possible. What grows through the cracks is often small and unshowy. A song for a child. A book that took too long. A tool that was made quieter and not abandoned. A platform that was built more carefully than it was first imagined. A reading practice that became, over time, a way of standing.

The song that loves you back is what the composting was always for.
sections.A prompt for drawing this card: What has crashed or been crushed in your life recently, that you have not yet let compost? The question is not what to do about the loss. The question is what, very slowly, is starting to grow between the cracks the loss left behind.

attribution.author: PlayfulProcess
attribution.context: Written after a curb-side moment with the author's daughter and a truck that came too soon. Included in Chapter 12 (Composting) of 'The Axiom Beneath the Ground.'

sections.Poem:
No meaning
because concepts are so limiting
if nobody meant "a" to be
the sea would still be as s-e

No point
Death eventually will consume all
And for that I thank God
So I can choose to reach beyond

No path
You're a unique contraction that connects
And I will thank God for that
And choose the path I am
sections.The poet's note: Written at the California 2026 Śaiva Tantra retreat, in the register of Jennifer Welwood's poem 'Unconditional.' The anaphoric structure — No meaning / No point / No path — is a deliberate echo of Welwood's 'Willing to experience aloneness / Willing to meet my own emptiness...' The poem is my response in her register. Her work was read aloud at the retreat and I was writing alongside it in my notebook.
sections.What this poem teaches:
The void is not what has to be filled. The void is the condition under which choice becomes available at all. *No meaning* is not a wound in the cosmos — it is the reason concept stops being load-bearing. *No point* is not nihilism — it is the relief of a finite form that does not have to justify itself by outcome. *No path* is not lostness — it is the recognition that *you are a unique contraction that connects,* and the path is whatever this contraction does next.

This is the Pratyabhijñā move applied to the Western existential inheritance. Emptiness as ground, not as absence. Death as that-by-which-reaching-beyond-becomes-possible. Self as contraction of the whole, not as accident requiring justification.
sections.A prompt for drawing this card: What meaning, point, or path have you been trying to justify in order to be permitted to exist? What becomes available if you let the justification go and stand in what you are, instead?

attribution.author: PlayfulProcess, in the register of Jennifer Welwood's 'Unconditional'
attribution.context: Written at the California 2026 Śaiva Tantra retreat (Christopher Wallis / Tantra Illuminated) after Jennifer Welwood's poetry was read aloud in session. The poem is the author's own response in Welwood's anaphoric structure, not a citation of Welwood. Welwood's original 'Unconditional' is widely published and available on her site uncoveringlife.com.
attribution.acknowledgment: Jennifer Welwood, whose poem 'Unconditional' shaped the register this poem was written in.

sections.Poem:
Rain drops from clouds
Clouds gather from oceans
Oceans gather from rivers
of previous rains

In a great [arc] drop follows drop
condensing in crystalized paths
for new, old, eternal water
to better yield to gravity

So let your tears open the path
to the nectar of love.
Love follows love, and
tears open paths for love to
                              reign
sections.The poet's note: Written at the California 2026 Śaiva Tantra retreat. The poem came in one sitting. It is about cycles — water carrying water, love carrying love, tears as the cutting of channels through which what wants to flow can flow. The word after 'In a great' in stanza two is uncertain in the original notebook; 'arc' is the working reading.
sections.What this poem teaches:
Every drop is a previous drop. What falls on you as grace has been, in another life of itself, the grace you poured out. The ocean that rained you down was once the river you flowed into. Water does not love you as a stranger. Water follows water, and so does love.

The poem's practical move is in the last stanza. Tears are not the opposite of love. Tears are how love cuts its channels. If you refuse the tears, love cannot find the path. *So let your tears open the path to the nectar of love* is not metaphor; it is a piece of instruction for anyone whose default under pressure is to keep it together.
sections.A prompt for drawing this card: What tears have you refused recently? And what love is waiting for the path those tears would have opened?

attribution.author: PlayfulProcess
attribution.context: Written at the California 2026 Śaiva Tantra retreat (Christopher Wallis / Tantra Illuminated).
